summary: Julia Evans explores the sendfile system call, explaining how it optimizes the common pattern of reading a file and writing it to a network socket by eliminating unnecessary data copies and context switches. She presents impressive performance benchmarks, including a Python FTP library doubling throughput and Netflix going from 6Gbps to 40Gbps on FreeBSD. However, she also covers the pitfalls, including a serious OS X bug that caused 5-second delays. The post connects sendfile to related system calls like splice and tee, and discusses practical configuration options in web servers like nginx and Apache.
key_points: sendfile eliminates the need to copy data from kernel to user space and back, reducing both CPU overhead and context switches when serving files over a network
Real-world benchmarks show dramatic improvements: 800MB/s to 1.5GB/s for Python FTP, and 6Gbps to 40Gbps for Netflix on FreeBSD
sendfile isn't universally safe — an OS X bug caused it to not send any data until the socket closed, which is why web servers let you toggle it on and off
sendfile is now a wrapper around the more general splice system call, which copies data between file descriptors without passing through user space
Understanding low-level system calls has practical implications for anyone configuring or building web servers that serve large files
money_quotes: So the idea is – this pattern of reading a file and writing to a socket is really common! So they made a system call to just do that!
That post describes how on OS X, sendfile wouldn't send any data until the socket was closed, causing up to 5 second delays. That's TERRIBLE.
So sendfile isn't some kind of universal panacea, and that's why webservers let you turn it on and off.
It's super interesting to me how much abstractions break down when you're trying to really optimize performance.
It makes me really happy when learning a new system call helps me understand how to do something really practical.
I straced cp on my machine just now, and it seems like it does not use sendfile.
key_insight: The sendfile system call can dramatically improve file-serving performance by keeping data in kernel space, but its platform-specific bugs remind us that even performance optimizations need to be tested and configurable.
